source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: An address from a learned man is always profitable, because the hearer either learns what he had known himself to be ignorant of, or, what is more, comes to know what he did not know he had been ignorant of.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: A hearer of the latter kind I have now become, your most holy Blessedness having been minded to write to me, asking me to send you the acts of all the martyrs, which were collected in the times of Constantine, of pious memory, by Eusebius of Cæsarea.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But before receiving the letter of your Blessedness I did not know of these acts, whether they had been collected, or whether not.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: I therefore give thanks that, instructed by your most holy teaching, I have begun to know what I was ignorant of.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For beside what is contained about the acts of the holy martyrs in the books of the same Eusebius, I am not aware of any collections in the archives of this our Church, or in the libraries of the city of Rome, unless it be some few things collected in one single volume.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: We have indeed the names of almost all the martyrs, with their passions assigned to particular days, collected in one volume; and we celebrate the solemnities of mass on such days in commemoration of them.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Yet it is not indicated in this volume who each was, and how he suffered; but only his name, the place, and day, of his passion are put down.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Hence it results that many of divers countries and provinces are known to have been crowned with martyrdom, as I have said, through their several days.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But these we believe you have.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: That, however, which you wish to have sent to you we have sought for, but have not found; but, though we have not found it, we will still search, and, if it can be found, will send it.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: With regard to what you write about the timber being short in length, the cause was in the kind of ship by which it was sent; for, if a larger ship had come, we could have sent larger pieces of timber.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But as to your saying that, if we send larger pieces, you will pay for them, we thank you indeed for your liberality, but we are precluded from accepting a price, since the Gospel forbids it.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For we do not buy the timber which we send; and how can we accept a price, when it is written, Freely ye have received, freely give (Matth. x.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: We have therefore sent now through the shipmaster timber of short length in accordance with the size of the ship, whereof a notice is subjoined.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Next year, however, should it please Almighty God, we will prepare larger pieces.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: We have received with the kindliness wherewith it was sent the blessing of Saint Mark the Evangelist, nay, it may be said more truly, of Saint Peter the Apostle; and, greeting you well, we beg your Blessedness to deign to pray for us, that so we may be counted worthy to be soon delivered from present evils, and not to be excluded from future joys.
